{{Short description|Physics concept: quantitative measure of inertia and gravitational interaction}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Mass
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Newton's laws of motion]], [[Force]], [[Energy]], [[Gravitation]], [[Special relativity]]
| symbol = m
| SI_unit = [[kilogram]] (kg)
| extensive = yes
}}

'''Mass''' is a fundamental property of [[matter]] that determines its resistance to [[acceleration]] ([[inertial mass]]) and its [[gravitational attraction]] to other bodies ([[gravitational mass]]). In the [[International System of Units]] (SI), the unit of mass is the [[kilogram]] (kg).

The [[equivalence principle]] — the foundation of [[general relativity]] — asserts that inertial and gravitational mass are identical.

== Inertial mass ==
Inertial mass is a measure of an object's resistance to changes in its [[velocity]] when a [[force]] is applied:
:<math>m_i = \frac{F}{a}</math>

This is the mass appearing in [[Newton's second law]]:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}</math>

== Gravitational mass ==

=== Active gravitational mass ===
Determines the strength of the [[gravitational field]] generated by an object:
:<math>\mathbf{g} = -\frac{G m_a}{r^2}\hat{\mathbf{r}}</math>

=== Passive gravitational mass ===
Determines the force an object experiences in a gravitational field:
:<math>\mathbf{F} = m_p\mathbf{g}</math>

== Mass–energy equivalence ==
{{Main|Mass–energy equivalence}}
In [[special relativity]], mass and [[energy]] are equivalent:
:<math>E = mc^2</math>

The relativistic mass increases with velocity:
:<math>m_{\text{rel}} = \gamma m_0 = \frac{m_0}{\sqrt{1 - v^2/c^2}}</math>
where <math>m_0</math> is the [[rest mass]].

== Mass of elementary particles ==
In the [[Standard Model]], the masses of [[elementary particle]]s arise through the [[Higgs mechanism]]. The [[photon]] and [[gluon]] are massless; the [[W and Z bosons]] acquire mass through [[electroweak symmetry breaking]].

== Conservation ==
In classical physics, mass is conserved in closed systems ([[conservation of mass]]). In relativity, mass–energy together is conserved. In nuclear reactions, mass can be converted to energy (or vice versa): [[nuclear fission]] and [[nuclear fusion]].

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Energy]]
* [[Force]]
* [[Gravitation]]
* [[Inertia]]
* [[Kilogram]]
* [[Mass–energy equivalence]]
* [[Weight]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Mass| ]]
[[Category:Physical quantities]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Matter]]
